My preference is that Message class would be updated to officially include a reference to the related gateway. Setting the attribute for the callback was a way to limit the scope of my proposed changes.

The issue is that the gateway constructor takes the event handler as a constructor argument. If the handler constructor needs a reference to the gateway (for resolving gateway-specific constants, for example) there is a messy circular dependency. This would be a scenario where the user has created a stateful event callback (a callable object or a bound function on an object with state) versus a simple function callback. There are workarounds. With the current implementation, one could not use the event_handler constructor argument and set the event_handler on the gateway after it is created. Or one could set the gateway on an event handler object after the gateway is created. Or the handler could access the gateway as a global variable, but that would prevent a generic handler from being used with multiple gateways. It seemed cleaner to me to give the callback access to the gateway that processed the incoming message. However, if you prefer one of the workarounds instead I can remove the related code from the PR.

Actually, I since modified this very condition to make sure it *never* returns True.
The lambda is used by Jinja2 loader to determine when to reload the templates.
If we do the right thing, given the interface, which would be `options == self.mapping.get(template)` what we end up with is *always* the same template every time (same random sentence always chosen). This openly works against what we're trying to do.

Instead, we should just use `lambda: True`, to signal that we always want Jinja to reload the template, thus picking a random one among the possibilities.

An alternative to sending an event here is to call CancelIoEx.  If it is used then the read event can be local to the run function.  It would also make the code a little more correct, since even after an IO operation is canceled, it can still have a side effect and write to the overlapped structure it was passed.  To make sure the operation has been canceled, the code needs to wait until the event is signaled by ReadFile.  If the operation was canceled then the result should be ERROR_OPERATION_ABORTED.

One downside with this is that CancelIoEx on exists on Windows Vista and above, so this would not run on XP machines.  Let me know what you think.  I could also make the modification in another PR in the future.  As the code is right now, it resolves all the issues I was seeing.

Ah yeah, docker-compose. There's a reason for this. 

In earlier versions of this PR, the build logs were massive, because, by default, docker-compose shows ascii progress bars when downloading docker images. These progress bars don't render properly in TravisCI (it becomes like 100 lines per progress bar). The only way to disable this is to [set the docker-compose log-level to `ERROR`](https://github.com/apache/incubator-airflow/pull/3393/files#diff-354f30a63fb0907d4ad57269548329e3R55).

This change was only added in docker-compose [1.20.0](https://github.com/docker/compose/blob/master/CHANGELOG.md#all-formats-1). The TravisCI version is very old (1.4 if I remember correctly).

Wait, if it turns out that you ended up sifting down, that means that you don't need to sift up, right?

Like if you have something where the heap property is already true, and then you change an item so that it is bigger than its children. `down` will swap it with the smaller of its two children. Now that new value at the index of the changed value doesn't need to be sifted up because we know that the heap property was true before, so the new value is greater than the parent of the value that changed.

Like if you have,

              i-1/2               i            i*2+1   i*2+2
    x = [..., parent_parent, ..., parent, ..., child1, child2, ...]

The heap property is already true so

    parent_parent < parent
    parent < child1
    parent < child2

which implies,

    parent_parent < child1
    parent_parent < child2

If you now change parent to violate the heap property so that it gets sifted down, that is, you change parent to be > child1 or child2, then,

              i-1/2               i            i*2+1   i*2+2
    x = [..., parent_parent, ..., child1, ..., parent, child2, ...]

Since only `parent` changed, `parent_parent < child1` is still true, so you don't need to `up(h, i)` anymore.

The `column` variable is passed by the user so I was allowing for them to do as they please. The HappyBase concept of `fam:` or `fam:col` is different from that of Cloud Bigtable.

In Cloud Bigtable the fields are separate: [`Column.qualifier`][1] (equivalent of `col` in `fam:col`) is `bytes` and [`Family.name`][2] (i.e. `fam`) is a string.

It's unclear what the right thing here is. For example if `fam` is a string but `col` has non-ascii bytes, then decoding to `utf-8` may be a bad idea.

[1]: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/cloud-bigtable-client/blob/7c77c170c8818a0440e0d2f4ecedcb8329c1f8d9/bigtable-protos/src/main/proto/google/bigtable/v1/bigtable_data.proto#L58
[2]: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/cloud-bigtable-client/blob/7c77c170c8818a0440e0d2f4ecedcb8329c1f8d9/bigtable-protos/src/main/proto/google/bigtable/v1/bigtable_data.proto#L45

It's important to strip whatever's returned by `read()` as a precaution. Let's say I create a file like so:

    echo 0.18.0 > foo

There's a trailing newline in the file:

```python
>>> with open('foo') as handle:
...     handle.read()
... 
'0.18.0\n'
```

I think this entire block of code can be dropped. `entity` is not used anywhere else in this method, so there's no need to instantiate a `HostGroup` and assign it to `entity`. `super` will do that. Also, imagine the following use case:

```python
from nailgun import entities
host_group = entities.HostGroup(id=5).read()
```

After `HostGroup(id=1)` finishes executing, a `HostGroup` object will be instantiated, and that object will have just one public instance attribute set: `id`:

```python
>>> from nailgun import entities
>>> host_group = entities.HostGroup(id=5)
>>> host_group.get_values()
{'id': 5}
>>> vars(host_group).keys()
dict_keys(['_server_config', '_fields', '_meta', 'id'])
```

When `.read()` is executed, `self.name` is referenced. However, as you can see, no such public instance attribute exists.

You can use an async test function to test a synchronous function :-).

```python
async def test_...():
    async with trio.open_nursery() as nursery:
        # This runs 'sync_fn' in a background thread
        nursery.start_soon(trio.run_sync_in_worker_thread, sync_fn)
        # ... we can do other stuff here while it's running ...
    # When we de-dent to close the nursery, it automatically joins the background thread
```

So this automatically makes sure that you join the background thread (it can't accidentally keep running while other tests go, even if this test crashes), it automatically detects exceptions in the background thread and makes sure that if they happen it causes the test to fail, and it's actually simpler to use (IMO) than the raw `threading.Thread` API :-).

In the (unlikely?) case that the `response` of a `HEAD` request does *not* contain a `Content-Length`, we'll end up sending no `Content-Length` header at all in the final response. Not sure if this is the desired behavior.

To be on the safe side, we could change it to something like this:

```
content_length_sent = False
for header_key, header_value in iteritems(response.headers):
    is_content_length_header = header_key.lower() != 'Content-Length'.lower()
    if is_content_length_header or method == 'HEAD':
        self.send_header(header_key, header_value)
        content_length_sent = content_length_sent or is_content_length_header
if not content_length_sent:
    self.send_header('Content-Length', '%s' % len(response.content))
```
